name: honor (honor)
shortDescription: Honor, in Roman mythology, was the divine personification of honor and glory. He was closely associated with Virtus, the personification of virtue and courage, and together they represented the ideal Roman citizen and warrior.
description: Honor, in Roman religion and mythology, was the divine embodiment of honor, glory, and public esteem. He represented the qualities most valued in Roman society, particularly among the elite. Honor was not a major deity with a complex mythology, but rather a personification of abstract concepts crucial to Roman identity and social order. Temples dedicated to Honor were often built alongside those of Virtus (virtue, courage), reflecting the interconnectedness of these ideals. The pursuit of honor through military achievement, public service, and adherence to traditional Roman values was a central theme in Roman life. He was considered a crucial aspect of maintaining Roman power and prestige, both internally within society and externally in relations with other nations and peoples. While specific myths dedicated solely to Honor are scarce, his presence is implied in stories emphasizing the importance of upholding one's reputation and achieving glory through virtuous actions. The concept of honor was also tied to the family name (nomen) and the importance of maintaining its reputation through generations.

sources.title: The Oxford Classical Dictionary
sources.author: Hornblower, Simon and Antony Spawforth, eds.
